brief.what_they_do: Maker of specialized equipment for the 'rock-to-road' industry — asphalt and concrete plants, road-building and aggregate-processing equipment, plus aftermarket parts and a digital/controls platform; two segments: Infrastructure Solutions and Materials Solutions. [SE-PR]

company_brief.the_view: A clean top-line beat masking a sharp profit miss — revenue grew 20% to $396.3M [SE-PR] but adjusted EPS fell to $0.54 from $0.91 [SE-PR] and badly missed the $0.88 Street bar [SE-SS Sidoti], driving a price-target cut to $64 from $69 [SE-SS Sidoti]. The growth is acquired, not organic (Materials Solutions +70.6% on the acquisitions vs Infrastructure Solutions +0.4% [SE-PR]); the miss is margin — gross margin fell 310bps as acquired mix + integration costs diluted the core. Backlog is the bull's anchor: $549.2M, +36.4% y/y [SE-PR]. Three things that matter: (1) GM compression is the story — 25.0% vs 28.1%, (310)bps [SE-PR]; the OP walk shows revenue +$67M almost fully eaten by COGS +$60M and SG&A +$18M. (2) The beat is M&A — Materials Solutions doubled on acquisitions; the legacy Infrastructure business was flat [SE-PR] — quality-of-growth question. (3) Backlog +36% + a possible highway-bill catalyst is the forward bull case [SE-PR][SE-CT] against a soft-margin present. Watch: whether Q2 margins inflect (CFO guided 'stronger margins in Q2 than Q1' [SE-CT]) and whether infrastructure order releases wait on a surface-transportation reauthorization [SE-CT].
company_brief.pnl: Revenue $396.3M (+20.3%); Adj EBITDA $23.6M vs $27.5M; Adj EPS $0.54 vs $0.91; GAAP diluted $0.06 [SE-PR]
company_brief.guidance: Five-year framework reaffirmed; Q2-26 margin 'stronger than Q1' with full normalization 2H; backlog $549.2M (+36.4%) [SE-CT]
company_brief.sell_side: Sidoti: PT cut to $64 (from $69), 2027 EPS to $3.98 (from $4.33) on the 1Q miss [SE-SS Sidoti]

room.note: Pankaj's 1on1 is with CFO Brian Harris (the CEO, Jaco van der Merwe, is NOT in this room). IR head Steve Anderson is also in the room.

people.name: Brian J. Harris
people.title: Chief Financial Officer
people.tells_from_call: On the Q1 call, Harris was the price-cost explainer. He reframed the margin miss by anchoring on the comp — '...if you go back to that Q1 of 2025, we had a gross margin of over 28%... that was a little bit of an outlier' — and stressed tariffs only 'began to materialize till April and beyond' last year, so 2025 was an easy compare, not 2026 a collapse [book]. He volunteered the FIFO point analysts raised, committed that backlog pricing 'should accommodate our anticipated inflation and tariff increases,' and gave a concrete Q2 steer: 'we're going to emerge with stronger margins in the second quarter than we saw in the first' [book]. A measured, balance-sheet-forward CFO who guides on leverage and conversion, not hype. [book]
last_q_commitments: Reaffirmed FY26 adj EBITDA $170–190M; tax 25–28%; capex $40–50M; D&A $55–65M; quarterly SG&A $70–80M, interest ~$7M [book]
Q2 margins to be stronger than Q1; additional pricing in the pipeline to mitigate tariff/freight [book]
Year-end net leverage ~1.7x at guidance midpoint, within the 1.5–2.5x target (ended Q1 at 2.3x) [book]
FCF conversion to stay strong; working-capital efficiency improving (inventory down vs year-end) [book]

smart_questions.q: Q2 margin inflection — the CFO promised Q2 margins 'stronger than Q1,' with full normalization in 2H. How much y/y margin pressure persists into Q2, and what specifically drives the 2H crossover — pricing in backlog, mix reverting, or the ConExpo one-off rolling off?
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a dated crossover + the pricing/FIFO mechanics behind it. Evasive: 'we price to anticipated cost' generically → the lag is longer than the guide assumes.

smart_questions.q: The +20% revenue was acquired (Materials Solutions +70.6%) while Infrastructure was flat at +0.4% and lost 350bps of op margin — and Materials runs a structurally lower 5.6% margin. Where does synergy realization stand, and when does the acquired growth stop diluting consolidated GM?
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a synergy run-rate + a timeline and the mix path back. Evasive: 'integration on track' with no number → the dilution is the story, not a phase.

smart_questions.q: Backlog is +36% but order releases are tied to a surface-transportation (highway-bill) reauthorization the company doesn't control. How much of the backlog conversion is policy-gated, and are customers holding POs until the bill is in place?
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a split of firm vs release-gated backlog + customer-order color. Evasive: leaning on the bill to backfill near-term softness → 2027 estimates rest on legislation.

granite_pov.line: A margin-timing miss with intact demand — revenue grew 20% but OP fell 56% as acquired mix + integration drove a 310bps GM compression and a 41% adj-EPS decline, while management reaffirmed the five-year framework on a record +36% backlog. The thesis turns entirely on a 2H margin inflection that is promised, not delivered. Pressure-test the Q2-stronger-than-Q1 steer, the synergy cadence, and how policy-gated the backlog conversion is.

brief.what_they_do: Global maker of agricultural equipment — high-horsepower tractors, combines, and precision-ag technology (brands: Fendt, Massey Ferguson, Valtra, PTx). [book]

company_brief.the_view: A big Q1 earnings beat in a still-soft ag market — adj EPS $0.94 vs $0.44 consensus [book], on sales of $2,342.9M (+14.3% y/y, but only +4.7% ex-FX) [book]. The beat was driven by strong Europe (EME) margins, FX, a lower tax rate, and channel revenue that ran ahead of end-user demand — i.e. quality is mixed, not pure demand. Management RAISED the FY26 adj-EPS outlook to ~$6.00 (from $5.50–6.00) [book], hiked the dividend to $0.30, and announced $350M of Q2 buybacks. Yet the stock reaction was negative — the debate is whether the 1Q beat is durable or front-loaded, against a higher tariff bill (~$135M in 2026) and a softer Q2.

smart_questions.q: How much of the 1Q adjusted EPS beat ($0.94 vs $0.44) was channel revenue that 'ran ahead of end-user demand,' and does that reverse in 2H — i.e. did you pull forward sales rather than see demand inflect?
smart_questions.why: Brokers flagged rev-rec timing as part of the beat; this is the durability question. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a hard number on the pull-forward + a sign demand actually inflected. Evasive: 'timing normalizes through the year' with no figure → the beat was borrowed from 2H and reverses.

smart_questions.q: The FY26 tariff cost jumped to ~$135M (+$25M vs your prior estimate) on the Section 232 change. What's the offset path — price, sourcing, or margin absorption — and is $135M now conservative or still moving?
smart_questions.why: Tariffs are the rising headwind partly offsetting the beat in the bridge. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a concrete offset bridge (price X, sourcing Y) and '$135M is our conservative number.' Evasive: 'we're monitoring it' → the headwind is still open and the guide is at risk.

smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a structural reason EME holds (mix, Fendt, cost program) + a dated NA inflection. Evasive: leaning on FX/comps → the margin is cyclical, not structural, and concentration risk is real.

smart_questions.q: You raised the dividend and announced $350M of Q2 buybacks plus a ~$190M JV stake sale to Rabobank funding repurchases — how should we think about capital-return pace vs. reinvestment given still-soft demand?

smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a clear capital-allocation framework + whether the JV sale is repeatable. Evasive: vague on pace → buyback is opportunistic optics, not a committed lever.

smart_questions.q: Retail tractor units are still down in NA and Brazil. What's your read on when the ag down-cycle troughs, and what's the first sign you'd point us to?
smart_questions.why: The cycle call underlies every estimate; ask the CFO for the leading indicator. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a specific leading indicator he watches (dealer inventory weeks, used-equipment pricing, order book). Evasive: 'hard to call' → no visibility, and 2026 estimates rest on faith.

granite_pov.line: A high-quality franchise printing a low-quality-of-source beat: own the EME margin and structural-cost story, but the 1Q upside is FX + tax + channel timing, not a demand turn, and the tariff bill is climbing. The capital-return step-up (dividend + $350M buyback + JV monetization) is the cleanest positive. Watch the rev-rec reversal risk into 2H and the ag-cycle trough signal.

brief.what_they_do: US-based specialty metals producer — makes alloy, carbon and micro-alloy steel: special bar quality (SBQ) steel, seamless mechanical tubing and value-added manufactured components. Sells into four end-markets: Industrial, Automotive, Aerospace & Defense, and Energy. Single reportable operating segment; reports results by end-market. company_brief.the_view: A clean beat-and-improve print. Net sales rose 10% y/y to $308.3M and adjusted EBITDA jumped 39% to $24.6M (margin 8.0% vs 6.3%, +167bps) on higher shipments, better price/mix and fixed-cost leverage [book]. GAAP EPS $0.13 (vs $0.03) and adjusted EPS $0.18 (vs $0.07) [book] — the $0.18 lands exactly on the $0.18 Street estimate, and revenue $308.3M is in line with the ~$308M consensus [SE-SS]. The story is demand-led: the order book is up ~40% y/y (~90k more tons), A&D shipments more than doubled (tons +106%), Section 232 tariffs (50% on imported primary steel) are a structural tailwind, and a new bloom reheat furnace (150 t/hr vs 100 legacy) ramps in 3Q [book]. The watch-items: Q1 profit was helped by a $4.9M other-income line, energy costs stepped up y/y (favorable electricity contract expired May 2025), and the Q2 guide is only 'modestly higher' with price/mix flat until 2H. The Street is constructive — Overweight, $22.50 PT — yet the stock fell ~4% on the day [SE-SS].

brief.what_they_do: Designer and manufacturer of vehicle propulsion solutions — fully automatic transmissions and electrified propulsion for on-highway trucks, buses, motorhomes, defense vehicles and off-highway equipment. Now operates through two business units: legacy Allison Transmission and the Allison Off-Highway Drive & Motion Systems business acquired from Dana on January 1, 2026 (with a centralized Allison Central Group cost center).

company_brief.the_view: The first quarter as a combined company. Reported net sales nearly doubled to $1,406M (+84% y/y) on the addition of $673M from the acquired Allison Off-Highway unit, but the headline GAAP optics are messy: consolidated gross margin fell to 29% from 49% and GAAP EPS dropped to $1.33 from $2.23 (-40%) — almost entirely purchase-price-accounting noise (~$76M of inventory step-up + incremental D&A in COGS, $21-22M intangible amortization, $17M integration costs) [book]. Strip the noise and the operating story is solid: adjusted EBITDA $362M (+22%) at a 26% margin and adjusted diluted EPS $2.57 (+6%) [book]. Legacy Allison Transmission revenue was down 4% to $733M in soft on-highway/medium-duty markets, offset by a +64% Defense quarter [book]. Bull case: synergy capture ($120M run-rate target) + a 27-29% EBITDA-margin path + cash generation. Bear case: end markets remain 'troughy,' the acquired unit ran a segment operating loss of $(21)M, and net leverage is elevated post-deal.
company_brief.pnl: Net sales $1,406M (+84%, incl. $673M Off-Highway); Adj EBITDA $362M (+22%), 26% margin; GAAP EPS $1.33, Adj diluted EPS $2.57 (+6%) [book]
company_brief.guidance: FY26 reaffirmed: net sales $5,575-5,925M; net income $600-750M; Adj EBITDA $1,365-1,515M (~25% margin at midpoint) [book]
company_brief.sell_side: Wells Fargo Equal Weight, PT raised to $137 (from $127) on 8.5x 2027E EBITDA; 3% EBITDA beat but stock down 1.3% as a raise was expected next quarter [book]

room.note: Diversified propulsion/drivetrain manufacturer mid-integration of the Dana Off-Highway acquisition; the debate is synergy capture + end-market recovery + cleaning through PPA noise. Financials below are books-only.

brief.what_they_do: Digitally-native equipment rental platform serving the largest jobsites nationwide. Two core segments: Equipment Rental & Services Operations (full-service branch rental — the ~77% growth engine) and Equipment Sales (new/used equipment, much of it placed into the capital-light OWN Program where third parties own fleet EquipmentShare deploys and manages). Built around T3, a proprietary operating system that ties telematics, access control, service and utilization data into one platform — the differentiation pitch on megaprojects (data centers, manufacturing, infrastructure). Founded 2015, Columbia MO; IPO'd Jan 23, 2026.

company_brief.the_view: A clean beat-and-raise from a hyper-growth organic compounder. Total revenue $989M (+38%) with Rental Segment revenue $764M (+37%); Adjusted Core EBITDA $399M (+38%), beating the ~$325M Street bar by ~$74M, and consolidated gross margin actually EXPANDED ~109bps to 29.0% [book]. Management raised the full-year guide across the board — FY26 revenue to $5,147–5,575M and Adjusted Core EBITDA to $1,883–1,995M (+$70M at the midpoint) — on maturing branches and 'unprecedented' megaproject demand [book]. The catch: still a GAAP net loss of $(29)M and a $200M operating-cash burn (vs $51M PY), so the bull case is unit-economics-and-maturation (mature sites at 55% TTM EBITDA margin) while the bear case is the cash burn, the OWN-model complexity, and a July lock-up overhang — which is why the stock fell ~6% on a clean print.

granite_pov.line: Best-in-class traffic-led growth, de-risked; the debate has moved from 'can they grow' to 'is the ~58x multiple already paying for it' — with flat RLM the swing factor.

brief.sourcing: All financials [book] = CAVA FactSet book (Q1 2026: press release, transcript, slides, 10-broker sell-side). Numbers recomputed from the schedule. Sample proof for the Gemini-vs-Claude convergence; Q&A attributed at firm level (no fabricated names).

task1_pnl.title: Task 1 · P&L
task1_pnl.read: Clean beat on every line. The only non-beat is RLM (flat) — deliberate reinvestment, not a miss; the EPS dip is a tax optic (PBT +48%).

task2_guidance.title: Task 2 · Guidance (FY26, raised)
task2_guidance.posture: Conservative / hedged: SSS midpoint raised +150bps to 5.5% (4.5-6.5%) yet Q1 printed 9.7% and Q2-to-date tracks ~9.7% — the guide embeds an H2 deceleration management isn't yet seeing [book].

rows: Q1 traffic + menu; H2 moderate MSD [book]

task2_guidance.read: A sandbag: the raise (mid 5.5%) lags the ~10% run-rate by ~450bps. Management is banking conservatism + reinvestment, not signaling deceleration.

task3_segments.title: Task 3 · Segments / Channel
rows: 459 units (+20.2%); AUV $3.0M vs $2.3M 1st-yr target; FB&P 29.1% (20)bps, Labor 25.7% flat, Other OpEx +80bps (3P) [book]
Mix 39.9% vs ~36% PY; digital rev +1,080bps q/q; KDS investment [book]
task3_segments.read: The model is the brand — digital mix rising is optically dilutive but priced profit-neutral; don't read the Other-OpEx tick as pressure.

task4_kpis.title: Task 4 · KPIs
task4_kpis.read: Traffic 6.8% is the whole story — the highest-quality kind of comp and the hardest for peers to copy.

task5_call.title: Task 5 · Call & Q&A

prepared.b: Revs +32% to $438.3M; adj EBITDA +38% to $61.7M; SSS 9.7% on 6.8% traffic [book]
prepared.quote: "a clear industry leader… meet the moment for the modern consumer" — CEO Schulman

prepared.b: 20 net new units → 459 (+20.2%); Midwest entries (Cincinnati, St. Louis, Columbus); 2024 class still positive at 18 months — honeymoon NOT fading [book]
RLM flat 25.1% ($108.9M RLP); leverage offset by 3P-delivery mix + wage; digital mix 39.9% [book]
Glazed Salmon national launch (first seafood, ~100bps RLM headwind, 'penny-profit neutral'); shrimp in Nashville market test [book]
Zero debt, ~$296-403M cash; OCF $64.1M, FCF $15.5M (vs $2.7M PY) [book]

qa.concern: Is the 9.7% comp + traffic velocity sustainable into Q2 as macro headwinds build?
response: Q2-to-date tracking ~Q1 (~9.7%), above the raised guide [book]
FY 4.5-6.5% guide deliberately buffers tough comps + macro [book]
qa.takeaway: The guide is a protective buffer, not a forecast of deceleration — re-raise optionality.

qa.concern: Why is RLM flat (25.1%) despite a 9.7% comp and sales leverage?
response: De-leverage from 3P-delivery mix + 2% wage investment [book]
Conservative ~1.4% pricing to hold the value proposition [book]
qa.takeaway: Delivery friction caps near-term margin; strategy prioritizes share + value over price.

qa.concern: Do new-store comps fade after the honeymoon (the structural unit-model bear)?
response: 2024 class still positive at 18 months — no cliff [book]
NRO productivity >100%; AUV $3.0M vs $2.3M 1st-yr target [book]
qa.takeaway: The fade-bear is refuted — de-risks the growth algorithm, which matters to the multiple more than the print.

qa.concern: Is the comp broad-based or concentrated in a few regions/cohorts?
response: Strength across all regions, vintages, and income bands [book]
Lower-income cohort outperforming (k-shaped) [book]
qa.takeaway: Breadth signals durable demand, not a one-market spike.

qa.concern: Why not take more price, and is salmon a permanent traffic shift?
response: Chose NOT to price beyond ~1.4% — the AB1228 precedent; traffic outperformed [book]
Salmon runs ≥4 quarters, not yet committed to everyday [book]
qa.takeaway: Pricing power is held in reserve (a future lever); salmon permanence stays open.

task5_call.read: The call dismantled the honeymoon-fade bear and reframed the debate as margin + valuation — management is reinvesting the comp upside, not banking it, and sandbagging the guide.
